channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Israel and Lebanon Agree to Renew Cease-Fire
item.description: The U.S.-brokered agreement called for “a complete cessation” of fire from Hezbollah, an Iranian-backed militia that operates in southern Lebanon. A previous cease-fire has largely been ignored.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Daniel Holler, State Department chief of staff, speaking during a meeting between Israeli and Lebanese delegations hosted by the United States, at the State Department in Washington on Wednesday.

item.title: Amid War, Iran’s Soccer Leader Works to Get His Team to the World Cup
item.description: Mehdi Taj said his federation has been dealing with FIFA, not the United States, in trying to obtain visas for Iran to play in the tournament that begins next week.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mehdi Taj being interviewed by a reporter in Tehran last month. Mr. Taj said mixed messages from Washington had created confusion over Iran’s participation in the World Cup.

item.title: South Korea’s Governing Democratic Party Sweeps Local Elections but Faces Setback in Seoul Mayor’s Race
item.description: President Lee Jae Myung’s Democratic Party had hoped to consolidate its influence and extend his political momentum.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:description: Democratic Party members and lawmakers, gathered at the National Assembly in Seoul on Wednesday, cheered after exit polls predicted a decisive win for the party in local elections.

item.title: Ukraine Strikes St. Petersburg on First Day of a Marquee Putin Event
item.description: As an annual economic conference was set to begin, President Volodymyr Zelensky said Ukraine had targeted a navy base and an oil terminal in the region that includes Russia’s second-largest city.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko and Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:description: Smoke rising from the port of St. Petersburg, Russia, on Wednesday. Ukraine has been expanding a campaign of long-range strikes aimed at inflicting economic damage on Russia.

item.title: Protests in UK Over Henry Nowak Murder Case Turn Violent, as Leaders Urge Calm
item.description: Eleven officers were wounded in clashes with demonstrators after right-wing politicians and commentators accused the police of anti-white bias.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Protesters and riot police officers in Southampton, a city in southern England, on Tuesday night.

item.title: Fire at a New Delhi Hotel Kills at Least 21
item.description: Dozens more were injured, the authorities said. The blaze appears to be the deadliest in the Indian capital in at least four years.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli and Hari Kumar
item.category: Flourish Stay B&B (New Delhi, India, Hotel)
item.media:description: Fighting the flames in New Delhi on Wednesday. The city’s fire department said that more than 40 people had been rescued from the building, which was in a residential area in the southern part of the capital.

item.title: For Dubai’s Migrants, War Is One More Worry in Super Stressed Lives
item.description: For laborers in Dubai, a free stress-management class offers a temporary oasis of calm amid struggles with debt, loneliness, long hours and, in recent months, the fear of missile strikes.
item.dc:creator: Mansi Choksi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Natalie Naccache for The New York Times
item.media:description: Laxmi Parekh has been running mental health workshops for Dubai’s migrant workers the past two years. Recently, she has incorporated the regional conflict into her lessons.

item.title: Navy Helicopter Crash Kills Three in Britain
item.description: The helicopter was on a training exercise when it crashed in a field in southwestern England.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:description: The site of a Royal Navy helicopter crash in Sourton Down, near Okehampton, England, on Wednesday.

item.title: The New Zealand Parakeet Pair That Are Saving Their Species
item.description: There are about 450 orange-fronted parakeets left in the world. Super breeders Nacho and Trixie are doing their best to bolster the population.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Leigh Percasky/The Isaac Conservation and Wildlife Trust, via AFP — Getty Images

item.title: Russia Launches Deadly Strikes on Kyiv After Threatening Ukraine for a Week
item.description: Moscow’s repeated warnings of a major strike, combined with the delay before it happened, seemed intended to inflict a psychological toll on the Ukrainian capital.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova and Kim Barker
item.media:description: The aftermath of a Russian strike on a residential building in Kyiv, Ukraine’s capital, on Tuesday.

item.title: U.S. Ebola Unit Plans in Kenya, Subject of Protests, Suffers New Setback From Court Ruling
item.description: The court further delayed the Trump administration’s proposed quarantine unit for Americans exposed to the virus. The plan has sparked angry protests in Kenya.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg and Brian O. Otieno
item.media:description: Demonstrators in Nanyuki, Kenya, on Monday, protesting plans to create an Ebola quarantine center for Americans.

item.title: U.S. Was Asked to Blacklist Colombian Cartel Gold. It Was Also Buying It.
item.description: The request for sanctions, from Colombia’s defense minister, shows failures at both ends of the supply chain for illegal gold.
item.dc:creator: Justin Scheck
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pedro Sánchez, center, Colombia’s defense minister, visiting an illegal mining operation in and around a military base last month.

item.title: Bombs Raining Down on Ukraine Can’t Hide Signs of Russia’s Faltering War
item.description: The war has not been going the Kremlin’s way, with battleground losses and growing casualties. With fiercer strikes, Moscow hopes to gain a better position for negotiations.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.media:description: Smoke rising from buildings in Kyiv on Tuesday, after a Russian missile and drone attack.

item.title: Four New Zealand Lawmakers Banned From China After Taiwan Trip
item.description: New Zealand’s foreign minister was “surprised” to hear about the one-year travel ban, a spokesperson said, given lawmakers have visited Taiwan for years without issues.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Presidential Office of Taiwan, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Vice President Bi-khim Hsiao of Taiwan, in red, posing for a group photo with, from left to right, the New Zealand lawmakers Laura McClure, Maureen Pugh, Duncan Webb and David Wilson at the presidential office in Taipei, Taiwan, in May.

item.title: Israel and Lebanon Renew Cease-Fire, Calling for Evacuation of Hezbollah Operatives
item.description: The agreement depends on the cooperation of Hezbollah, the Iran-backed militia. The cease-fire could remove an obstacle in the talks between the United States and Iran.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Daniel Holler, the State Department chief of staff, speaking during a meeting between Israeli and Lebanese delegations in Washington on Wednesday.

item.title: Israel’s High Court Rejects Ban on Red Cross Visits for Palestinian Prisoners
item.description: Israel forbade the visits, which allow the Red Cross to monitor prisoners’ conditions, in 2023. Justices said the policy violated Israeli and international law.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
International Committee of the Red Cross
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the Red Cross in Gaza last year.

item.title: Trump Says Iran War Is ‘Not a Big Thing’ for U.S.
item.description: It was the latest attempt by President Trump to flip the narrative on a conflict that he once said would lead to quick and decisive victory.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump at the Oval Office on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Agitates Brazil’s Politics, Again
item.description: President Trump appears ready to lend a hand to a new right-wing ally ahead of a crucial election this year that will be the biggest test for Latin America’s left.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova
item.media:description: Flávio Bolsonaro in Brasília following his meeting with President Trump at the White House.

item.title: Africans Exploring Africa
item.description: A growing middle class is increasingly mobile — and changing African ideas about leisure, travel and even the continent itself.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Carmen Abd Ali for The New York Times

item.title: Four Immigrant Workers Burned to Death in Italy
item.description: The immigrants had been working as fruit pickers in southern Italy, the authorities said, apparently as part of a system of criminal labor trafficking.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni and Josephine de La Bruyère
item.media:description: Surveillance footage showed two people dousing a van with fuel and blocking the doors as the vehicle and its passengers burned in Calabria, Italy, on Monday.

item.title: Iranian Drone and Missile Barrage Against Kuwait Inflames Regional Tensions
item.description: One person was killed and dozens were injured at Kuwait’s international airport. Tehran denied it had struck the airport.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim, Leo Sands, Yan Zhuang and Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A photograph released by Kuwaiti state media showed officials inspecting damage in a terminal at Kuwait International Airport after an attack on Wednesday.

item.title: Travelers Bound for the U.K. Hit a Roadblock: Electronic Authorization System Is Down
item.description: Visitors from the U.S., Canada and most European countries can’t get an electronic travel authorization and are being turned away from planes, trains and boats.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.media:credit: K.C. Alfred/The San Diego Union-Tribune, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Some ticketed passengers have not been able to board flights bound for Britain because the electronic authorization system has been down.

item.title: As Ebola Outbreak Widens, Trump Has Yet to Outline a Plan
item.description: Despite long-established procedures for bringing Americans home for monitoring and treatment, the Trump administration has not said that it will allow those at risk of Ebola back into the country.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health workers wearing protective equipment walk through the isolation ward of a newly established Ebola treatment center in Ituri Province, in the Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: Unusual Greenpeace Lawsuit May Proceed, Dutch Court Says
item.description: Greenpeace International is arguing under Dutch law that an American pipeline company, Energy Transfer, sought to silence it with a costly lawsuit in North Dakota.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators against the Dakota Access Pipeline in North Dakota in 2016. The lawsuits are centered on Greenpeace’s involvement in the protests.

item.title: Alex Younger, Former Head of Britain’s MI6, Dies at 62
item.description: As director of the espionage agency from 2014 to 2020, he confronted a range of threats, including international terrorism and an emboldened Russia.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Alex Younger in 2018. He sought to demystify the work of MI6 and to broaden recruitment by an organization associated with fictional spies like James Bond.

item.title: Trump Administration Turns to a New Rationale to Justify Old Tariffs
item.description: The administration has settled on a more legally and politically durable way to impose tariffs, but some say the focus on forced labor laws is merely a pretext for protectionism.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:description: On Tuesday, the Trump administration announced a tariff of 10 percent to 12.5 percent on 59 countries and the 27-member European Union.

item.title: Trump Confirms He Called Netanyahu ‘Crazy’ and Hopes to Meet Iran’s Supreme Leader
item.description: President Trump said that he had been “perturbed” in a call with Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel, and said he hoped to meet with Iran’s supreme leader.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House last week.

item.title: Attack on Kuwait Airport Further Upends Mideast Travel Amid U.S.-Iran Conflict
item.description: Airports in Kuwait, Iraq, Bahrain, the U.A.E. and Iran, some with military functions and some without, have all come under fire.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Terminal 1 at Kuwait International Airport on Monday. Kuwait’s army said the terminal was damaged in an Iranian attack early Wednesday.

item.title: A Death at the Epicenter of Ebola
item.description: Our chief Africa correspondent, Declan Walsh, reports from the epicenter of the Ebola outbreak on how families, medical workers and local volunteers are grappling with losses of life.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh, Estelle Caswell, Thomas Vollkommer and Arlette Bashizi

item.title: Here’s where U.S. troops are stationed in the Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes

item.title: Attacks on Kuwait and Bahrain Add Further Strain to U.S.-Iran Cease-Fire
item.description: One person was killed in an attack on the international airport in Kuwait, local authorities said, as Iran continues to target U.S. allies in the Persian Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Damage at Kuwait International Airport on Wednesday, in a still image obtained from a social media video that was verified by The New York Times.

item.title: Ukraine’s Daily Moment of Silence
item.description: Every day at 9 a.m., Ukrainians observe a minute of silence to honor people killed during the Russia-Ukraine war. Our reporter, Maria Varenikova, who has covered the hostilities since 2014, takes us to her hometown to experience this moment.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova, Coleman Lowndes, Rebecca Suner and Joey Sendaydiego

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak, Vivian Nereim and Shirin Hakim

item.title: Trump Endorses Abelardo De La Espriella, Right-Wing Presidential Candidate in Colombia
item.description: Mr. Trump said that the candidate, Abelardo De La Espriella, was important for the U.S.-Colombia relationship and called his left-wing rival a “Radical Left Marxist.”
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Abelardo De La Espriella at his closing rally in Medellín, Colombia. He gave campaign speeches from a bulletproof booth.

item.title: War Games and Warnings on Strait of Hormuz Went Unheeded by Trump
item.description: Over the past two decades, Iran repeatedly threatened to close down the waterway. President Trump underestimated Iran’s ability to do so.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Iran’s control of the Strait of Hormuz has become a huge source of leverage in negotiations with President Trump.

item.title: U.S. Treasury Imposes Sanctions on Iran’s Biggest Crypto Exchange
item.description: The action against Nobitex and several of its executives accused it of helping the government evade sanctions and finance militants.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Treasury Department announced sanctions on Iran’s largest cryptocurrency exchange and others.

item.title: Hezbollah’s Drones
item.description: The militant group is looking more capable now than it did when Israeli forces marched into Lebanon three months ago. Here’s how it gained an edge.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Rubble from an Israeli strike carried out last week in Tyre, Lebanon, yesterday.

item.title: What to Know About South Korea’s Elections
item.description: President Lee Jae Myung came to office after his predecessor, a conservative, was ousted for imposing martial law. Now exit polls suggest that Mr. Lee’s popularity rippled across other contests.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:description: South Korean election officials carrying boxes containing ballots during elections in Seoul, South Korea, on Wednesday.

item.title: Toddler Dies After Bouncy Castle Is Lofted by Harsh Winds in Montreal
item.description: Severe thunderstorms lashed an annual church party, sending airborne an inflatable toy often found at outdoor parties, paramedics said.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai

item.title: Murder of Henry Nowak, Who Was Handcuffed by Police While Dying, Fuels Outrage in UK
item.description: The authorities in Britain are investigating police officers who handcuffed a student, Henry Nowak, while he was dying, in a case that been increasingly politicized.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:credit: Gareth Fuller/Press Association, via Associated Press
item.media:description: People gathering outside a police station in Southampton, England, to protest the death of Henry Nowak on Tuesday.

item.title: Protests Grow in Albania Over Kushner-Linked Project
item.description: Plans for luxury resorts on the Adriatic Sea have long worried environmentalists. The developments are now a flashpoint for broader frustrations with the government.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:description: Crowds gathered in Tirana, the capital of Albania, on Monday to protest a planned tourist resort along the Adriatic Sea.

item.title: Trump Again Refers to Canada as the ‘51st State’ Hours Before Trade Talks
item.description: The Canadian trade minister met with the U.S. Trade Representative on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: Dominic LeBlanc, Canada’s minister in charge of trade, is scheduled to meet with Jamieson Greer, the United States Trade Representative, in Washington on Tuesday.

item.title: Steph Curry and Li-Ning: What to Know About N.B.A. Star’s Deal With Chinese Sneaker Company
item.description: The agreement, which includes plans for sportswear and athleisure products, and stores across the United States and China, is the biggest involving an N.B.A. star and a Chinese brand.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:description: Stephen Curry of the Golden State Warriors during an April N.B.A. game.

item.title: Netanyahu’s Critics Accuse Him of Running Israel as a U.S. ‘Vassal State’
item.description: Critics pounced on the Israeli prime minister after President Trump claimed on social media he had persuaded him to call off “a major raid” on Hezbollah in Beirut.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel has faced criticism from his opponents after he appeared to call off an attack on Hezbollah in the Lebanese capital.

item.title: Iran Reveals 3-Day Funeral Plan for Ayatollah Khamenei, After Long Delay
item.description: Ceremonies for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei will be held in multiple cities, including Tehran, where authorities expect up to 20 million people to attend, Iranian state media reported.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands and Leily Nikounazar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Office of the Iranian Supreme Leader, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A photo released by the Office of the former Iranian supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khameini, in 2025.

item.title: ‘La La Land’ Orchestral Performance Saved by Keyboardist in the Audience
item.description: After a musician fell ill during a live performance of the score from “La La Land,” the composer Justin Hurwitz asked for a sight reader. A 21-year-old student stepped up.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses

item.title: Lebanon Endures Threats, Diplomacy and Whiplash as Others Decide Its Fate
item.description: The conflict between the United States, Israel and Iran looms over a state that has been pummeled by military strikes but has little say in determining its own future.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Inside a damaged hospital at the site of an Israeli strike in Tyre, Lebanon, on Tuesday.

item.title: Carney Says Canada Faces a ‘Crisis of Antisemitism’
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney’s comments followed calls for action from Jewish organizations and after a government report found that antisemitism had surged in Canada in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang
item.media:credit: Chris Young/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada speaking at Holy Blossom Temple in Toronto on Monday.

item.title: Battling a Deadly Ebola Outbreak in Eastern Congo
item.description: At the heart of the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo, health care workers are racing to open new treatment centers and ramp up testing. But after years of war, the deadly virus is just one of many challenges they’re facing.
item.dc:creator: Bethlehem Feleke, Michael Anthony Adams, Yasu Tsuji, Jon Hazell and Monika Cvorak

item.title: How Hezbollah Drones Changed Israel’s Strategy in Lebanon
item.description: An Israeli campaign that started with high hopes has devolved into a kind of impasse, with Hezbollah looking more capable than it did when the war began.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Residents scoured rubble in the aftermath of an Israeli airstrike in Tyre last month.

item.title: A Stray Dog Became a Cultural Symbol in Brazil. Now, Mexico Is Claiming It.
item.description: To Brazilians, the ubiquitous caramel-colored mutts are a national icon. But a Mexican state has adopted them as its own homegrown symbol.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova and María Magdalena Arréllaga
item.media:description: Tiana, a “caramelo,” at a park in Rio de Janeiro in April.

item.title: Trump Targets Brazil With 25% Tariff, Citing Unfair Trade Practices
item.description: The proposal marks the latest effort by the administration to rebuild its tariff agenda through Section 301 investigations.
item.dc:creator: Daisuke Wakabayashi
item.media:description: Bulk carriers at the port of Paranagua, Brazil, in March.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Christina Goldbaum and Michael Crowley

item.title: Security Council Members Call for Israel to Withdraw From Southern Lebanon
item.description: The U.N. panel met after Israel had threatened to attack southern Beirut. President Trump said later that Israel and Hezbollah had agreed not to attack each other.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.media:description: Fleeing the southern suburbs of Beirut, a family sheltered on the roadside Monday amid growing traffic, after Israel warned the area would be targeted.

item.title: As Ebola Spreads, Scientists Race to Find Vaccines and Treatments
item.description: The Bundibugyo virus, a little known type, previously had caused just two small outbreaks. Now it’s at the center of a rapidly widening epidemic in Africa.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health workers disinfected a coffin containing the body of a man presumed have died of Ebola in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
